replies.author: @DirtyShirt1914
replies.text: ​@thegreatervesselnamedkingp6150that definitely contextualizes his intent, I can see the parallels. The problem I think is the sheer indulgence and vanity of the project to create and change the landscape of CGI (undeniable, incredible visuals and experience, no doubt meteoric influence) results in boring transitions for the sake of showing off this incredible world that's been created and what the technology can do. It stunts any meaningful impact and stakes of the story they are trying to tell. It gets lost and becomes pandering and self-righteous and shows that all the effort was in the visuals and not enough effort was put into the story, which could have a lot more meaningful impact and been less boring. Dumbing things down repels audiences too. By trimming the vanity and making more but shorter stories they could have allowed us to explore the incredible world they created by engaging with it and the story instead of showing it to us as long boring transitions that disrupt the narrative flow and bore audiences. While I might have a short attention span, I also got shit to do, so 3hrs is excessive if it could have been shorter and had more impact. It takes away from all the hard work.

I feel like it is consistent with the rest of how he acts and thinks, though.

He is constantly caught between a tendency to be strict, angry, and brutally honest in a way that tends to blow up in his face, and a desire to bring his family together and protect them.

So, just backing off and deciding he took it too far seems like the safer bet (in his eyes) when it comes to satisfying the latter, especially because he often has moments where he's ashamed/regretful of the former.

Which in turn shows pretty clearly that even though his issue manifests a bit differently, he's really not all that different from Neytiri.  He's just as capable of letting his anger lead him into cruelty, and he knows it.

If you boil any series down that far, you're going to boil away what matters.

Tell me if you've seen this movie.  Skywalker and the gang are on the ropes, and it looks like the Empire have them beat.  But, in a spectacular special effects battle and with the help of an old wizard, they save their friends and live to fight another day.

Avatar makes sense when you realize that the story is told through world-building, not a skeletal list of events.  It's like LOTR in that sense.  You could see it as "some elf knocks over a vase and now we have to watch a 90 minute sword battle."  But the story and the mythmaking are in the setting, not the action.

replies.author: @JETZcorp
replies.text: ​@mikespearwood3914 Every movie is a re-hash.  Star Wars pulls from Greek and Biblical mythology, Jurassic Park is Frankenstein, etc.  Avatar pulls from dozens of different films and media.  It's Pocahontas, it's No Man's Land, it's Dune, it's Cyberpunk 2077, it's a lot of things.  And so is every other movie.  But even if originality is everything to you, does the world-building count for nothing?  Where else do you find humans remote driving the living body of an alien race?  Where else to people and animals and an entire ecosystem at large communicate with a physical neural link?  If that means nothing, then the Jedi and the Death Star mean nothing, because they're just samirai and HMS Dreadnought with special effects.

replies.author: @JETZcorp
replies.text:
​@mikespearwood3914 The characters are no less memorable than Dune.  Can you name Paul Atreides' mother right now?  How about the dude from the Harkkonen that he fights?  I can't.  But they're still brilliant movies (with the same EXACT plot as Avatar 1, btw) and no one calls them empty or with nothing to say.  Because no one told you that Dune was a meaningless Pocahontas in the desert, but that's all you've ever heard about Avatar.

Are the characters as iconic as Star Wars?  Of course not.  Those are the most iconic characters of the entire 20th century.  The OT are better films.  But, they weren't any more original than Dune ir Avatar or Jurassic Park, ALL of which are built on very basic plot structures and archetypes from the western film and literature canon.  Maybe you don't personally connect with the characters.  That's fine.  I don't care about a single person in Lord of the Rings, but I don't go around saying they have a bad story or no meaning just because that story and meaning don't connect to me.  But for some reason, the discourse around Avatar is all of this, "I was so distracted by the CGI that I didn't notice the story so therefore there's no story" shallow nonsense.  I honestly think you'd get the stiry better if they remade it in Roblox or a high school play or something.

replies.author: @mikespearwood3914
@JETZcorp
replies.text:
​@mikespearwood3914 You think I give a shit what the popular opinion is?  I've been arguing this since day one.

Also, these movies are actually very culturally relevant, just not on the English-speaking internet.  They're only medium performers in the domestic box office, but they DOMINATE in other markets around the world.  India particularly has an enormous and passionate Avatar fanbase.  But regardless, I don't need their opinion or yours to know that I getba lot from these movies, and I find something new every time I re-watch them.  Way of Water especially.

But perhaps it's just a storytelling style that's not for everyone.  I really get into immersion experiences where everything fits.  Avatar, Cyberpunk, Andor, Red Dead 2, etc.  The setting is the real story, and the events are within it.  I think a lot of people view stories purely as chains of events, with setting and world-building merely props.  If you're the latter, Avatar won't work for you, but a $95 budget stage production of The Godfather might.  And, I do recognize that a lot of people really do like Avatar only because there's cool shit going on.  My dad liked Rise of Skywalker - he's not thinking hard about what Kiri's seizures tell us about how Eywa works.  But I am.

replies.author: @JETZcorp
replies.text:
​@BertoxolusThePuzzled Again, you really have to appreciate that "the humans" in Avatar are only being represented by one cyberpunk mega-corporation.  The humans in Star Wars are the Empire, after all.  But there are humans on the Rebel side, too.  Well, that's Jake abd Grace and Trudy and Norm and Max.

A lot of these backgrounds details were left on the cutting room floor, unfortunately.  The first movie desperately wanted to be 4.5 hours long.  There was so much character development for Parker which showed that he was less of a mustache-twirling bad guy and really just an "assistant manager at Chili's" type who was in way over his head.  Which is exactly the kind of people who are actually responsible for most of the atrocities in the world.  I think it was a good move for Cameron to make the leader of the enemy faction be a middle manager who just wants to go home and get his quarterly bonus, rather than some Dr. Evil character who creates suffering for the love of the game.  It means you can't dismiss it as fantasy.  You probably don't know anyone like The Emperor, but you probably do know someone like Parker or Quaritch.  I think that makes a lot of people uncomfortable.

replies.author: @BertoxolusThePuzzled
replies.text:
​@JETZcorpI mean they could have easily made Avatar humanity essentially the space age version of the mid century German supremacists or the raiding and pillaging Mongol hordes just to provide two historical examples. Or better yet make them the pragmatic form of evil where yes they are supremacist mustache twirling villains but ALSO are legitimately driven by a pure desire to do what they see as necessary to secure the survival of the human race as a whole.

The simple fact that we are three movies in and don't even know the basics about human government structure or the actual condition of the Earth itself is itself a massive failure, I mean how can you spend around 9 hours of content with the humans being your antagonists and yet after all that time STILL present next to nothing about them? 

They treat them like a simple villain in terms of writing and development but then give them motivations that are legitimately morally ambiguous if not arguably morally correct, and never address the massive discontinuity that this causes.

replies.author: @JETZcorp
replies.text:
​@BertoxolusThePuzzled I agree, there was not enough exposition to show that the bad guys are the RDA specifically, and they're a massive reason that Earth is dying.  The "strong prey on the weak" deleted scene is excellent and I think keeping it would have gone a long way. If you're familiar with the Cyberpunk franchise, the RDA is almost exactly Arasaka, minus the Japanese veneer.  That deleted scene even takes place in an overcrowded cyberpunk mega city drenched in rain and neon.  I including think that would have gone a long way to making the tone less District 9 and more Blade Runner.  I definitely have disagreements with what was included in the final cuts and what has to be picked up from expanded media.

Also, I think they really stepped on a land mine by calling the mineral unobtainium.  Engineering people know that term since the 50s, but it sounds dumb to everyone else, and that probably sabotaged the franchise's credibility.  It could have been "superconducting polythemite" or something like that.

You can't show humans being this "evil",  AND technologically advanced enough to travel in space to other planets, to create human-DNA-infused space Smurfs, to control those Smurfs through brain machines, have all the medical advances shown in the movies, etc etc.

You're not telling me that "we" can't synthesize a gas or pathogen that is harmful to Na'vi, and not harmful to humans. Smoke that they can't breathe, diseases that only harms them, weapons that knock them out based on their genetic buildup.

Or, since everything on Pandora seems to be connected somehow and they rely on that, find a way to remove that connection, or use that connection. Like.. upload a "virus" or something into the big glowy god trees, and once they use their hair to connect to it, it fries their brains..

replies.text: I watched the second one yesterday just to see what this amazing CGI was about etc..  Maybe 10 years ago I'd have been like oh cool, but now it's not looking much better than a good Sora render to me. That's really all these movies are and Cameron didn't exactly hide that sentiment. He is basically trying to swing his tech nuts around like look what I can do etc. The story comes in at a far-behind lagging 2nd to that. It needed to be colorful, wondrous, unique, etc.. so there you have a planet like Pandora. It's 3 hours of extremely slow and boring top notch CGI that is supposed to mesmerize you every second because you're supposed to be thinking "omg how are they making this look so real" etc. Again, this is sort of a pointless gimmick when you can scroll social media nowadays and come across countless AI renders that have you question whether it's reality or not.

replies.author: @hungarianaustralian
replies.text: @heemstr What made the original Star Wars Trilogy so compelling was the same villain across all three films.  With the Prequel Trilogy we got Darth Maul, Count Dooku and General Grievous.  It was such a shame to throw away those characters so quickly and carelessly.  I personally love the pure self-indulgence on the part of James Cameron in bringing Quaritch back.  Sinking the whaling ship in the final act was more self indulgence as he basically sank the Titanic again.  We saw Jake Sully adapt to the lifestyle of the Omatikaya in the first film and seeing his family adapt to the lifestyle of the Metkayina in the second film was surprisingly satisfying and the relationships between the young people was well done.  But the move from the jungle to the sea and the stark contrast between the two environments was the best thing about The Way of Water.  Having said all of that, the third film made a fatal mistake when it revisited the jungle and the sea again and again.  I wanted to see much more fire and ash in Fire and Ash.  It would have been fascinating to see the characters take refuge from the combined threat of volcanoes triggered by mining and the ongoing whaling by retreating into vast underground caverns that were also largely underwater.  We could even have seen the antagonistic Mangkwan clan try to cooperate with the other two tribes.  So much potential wasted with a rushed rehash of beats from a movie we saw way back in 2009 and another movie we saw as recently as 2022.

Here's a better third movie:
- The Ash Tribe is suffering because Eywa wants them dead
- Quaritch comes to them and offers to help them with human technology- teach them farming, civilize them, etc. 
- The other Na'Vi tribes who are not as favored by Eywa take notice and form alliances with the humans as well
- Sully notices this and starts trying to recruit other Na'Vi as well
- This leads to violent clashes between the two factions of Na'Vi
- Quaritch realizes that Eywa is a real entity that controls everything on the planet and tries to free the Na'Vi from their willing slavery
- This causes religious schism across the planet as the Na'Vi consider how cruel Eywa had been.  Many sign up with Quaritch's faction
- Then end it all with a big battle where the Pro-Human Na'Vi win and drive out the Pro-Eywa Na'Vi, who are forced into hiding in a downer Empire Strikes back sort of ending

replies.author: @IceRain-f5v
replies.text:
Well to be fair he’s an avatar and a Chad so he can survive a lot more than some human white kid with dreadlocks, the fall was clearly never implied to be lethal for quaritch

What is more infuriating is that in this movie they had a big plot point about both Jake and quaritch saving spider together and bonding over it and...... they already did that last movie 
Like did they forget?? Why didn’t James Cameron continue the story line? He just delayed it by an entire movie even though he says he’s not sure to continue
WtH??

But the psychology and behavior of he characters is one of the good aspects of the movie. James Cameron knows a lot about psychology that’s how he’s able to reign as a tyrant over thousands of employees in a budget kd hundreds of millions, and how he’s able to make such successful movies. His understanding of human psychology. He’s similar to Kubrick and David Lynch in this. 

And these scenes, where one second characters are trying to kiII each other, and the next they’re having a friendly talk, are extremely realistic, that’s how it works in real life
Especially if the people went through common experience or trauma and bonded inbetween, or if the heat or justification of the events is gone. Tons of WW2 soldiers who became friends after the war was over and joked about how they shot each other. As in they were physically face to face during the war and remember it. 

Humans generally only kiII others in very specific circumstances and if those are gone then they can be friends again or forget why they were trying to kiII each other. That’s realistic. 

Let’s see each example
Neytiri and Jake
Neytiri is an eviI racist who kiIIs all humans and avatars because they are human. But she’s also a superstitious lunatic and the sapient computer Eywa which pretends to be a god tells her not to harm him but to become his gf instead. So she does to please her "god". 
Spider and quaritch. Spider originally tries to defend himself when initially attacked because it’s a combat situation. Later when things have cooled off he stops and even later saves his life because he’s his father. 

Quaritch and Jake. Jake absolutely has it coming and quaritch is fully justified, but when they have trouble lost together in the jungle they can put their differences behind them. After doing that a few times and having to save spider a few times, something they do because they’re both protective of him, they mutually save each other. Quaritch agrees to remove Jake's cuffs and even gives him a weapon, and later Jake convinces the bad guy Navis not to shoot Quaritch at the end of ash and fire. That’s realistic behavior once again. Quaritch initiated a fact because he an see himself growing a soft spot and is like "hey wtf are you doing don’t be a dumbass this guy is a selfish bastard who killed thousands go take him out" which is why he reverts to fighting after being peaceful and chill for a few seconds. He’s aware he can drift into friendship and that he definitely shouldn’t on a moral standpoint because of all the horrible stuff Jake did (refusing to transfer the trade offer from the RDA to the natives, causing the conflict, planning the attack on the human base, carrying it out, being a turncoat, being the cause for the destruction of both home tree and hells gate, then getting both the whales the sea clan and the sea traders into trouble knowingly). 

Lastly there’s Varang. Varang initially wants to kiII... everyone, including quaritch. He manages to survive her and impresses her in several ways which makes her curious. Then as she learns more about him, she’s like "omg he’s literally me", and quaritch thinks "omg she’s literally me", so they forget their precious priorities and become soulmates. Obviously Varang doesn’t want to kiII quaritch anymore. As for quaritch he literally never wanted to kiII anyone except Jake in the entire series he’s sort of a non violent pacifist when you take appart his actions, even if he looks like the military tough guy.  
Varang also agrees not to harm the others. Why? Because quaritch says so, and she trusts the judgement of her husband. She thinks highly of herself but she isn’t a delusional narcissist, her high ego is perfectly in proportion with her superior intellect and abilities. But Quaritch is an older more mature man and leader than she is, and she recognizes he may have superior experience in politics and diplomacy, and so just let’s him cook. Again, everyone behaves in a realistic manner. Character behavior is something the avatars movie do really well, honestly flawless. The characters may be flawed or dumb but that’s realistic, they never act out of character or unrealistically compared to who they are as people.

replies.author: @victorcates9330
replies.text:
Your english teacher would have to read your essay, so valued their own time. But yeah. A cutting room has a floor. So some genius had the idea of cutting sections of film out and depositing the reel on the floor. Anything about 2h needs to be good. 3h is another level. It's the feeling of an english teacher looking at the number of pages and feeling a pure hatred for his students. In year 9, my RE teacher didn't include a word limit and a couple of the nerds were very proud that their essays exceeded 10,000 words. Not that they had said anything interesting - but that their essays could be printed out and you'd have a tome so thick you could club an ox to death with it.

The terminator was a normal film length. t2 and Alien were longer than 2 hours. The abyss was considered longer than it needed to be (crossing the line from masterful slowing down to increase tension into "oh my god, why is this sequence still happening?". Things were getting away from Cameron in the late 80s. A taut 90 minutes might be perfunctory. You might be able to slow down and wring more tension to get a film around the 2h mark. And this was Cameron's bread and butter. Aliens was used in my film class at university as an example of pacing. But he would have made it worse if Ripley had to fetch Newt an additional 6 times.

I don't really understand the hate that the movies get though. I do like them and think they're well-made, other than some of the fantastical stuff that happens like when certain characters should have died but miraculously survive, that kind of takes you out of the immersion for a few moments if you question it. Gotta hand it to James Cameron though for stretching the story out this far already and not having it completely fall flat on its face. I'm interested to see the final outcome of this adventure and I'll actually be sad when it's all over and done with. I think the series has a lot of depth and they are good action movies, with a tried and true story format but whatever, I can't name off the top of my head any other decent movies that have come out recently 🤷🏻‍♂️

The most frustrating thing about this (aside from getting to see the cool human tech and vehicles do cool things less and less in each film) is that fundamentally everyone likes to cheer for the underdogs in stories... but the humans are _by far_ the underdogs here. They're already physically much smaller/weaker and can't breathe the air... and then on top of this all their advanced helicopters with guns and long-range guided missiles just sort of... float there not killing anyone until they get shot down by one arrow or thrown into a rock by a pterodactyl.

This one was even worse for this. The humans show up with more firepower than even in the first film, and just... sit there dying while barely killing more than, what, maybe two or three navi? But then the fire navi show up (an even fight aside from their guns) and it's instantly "oh no we've definitely lost and are all going to die". This is NOT how you write factions' "power levels" if you want the navi to be sympathetic.

replies.author: @captain_buggles
replies.text:
Tbh looking at real life it's not that insane to me.

A lot of military tech and equipment is basically one big overpriced grift to benefit billionaires, a lot of shit goes for sounding cool and impressing investors over being practical, and the whole system's rife with nepotism.

Give all that like 150 more years to fester, and of course the military's mostly a shiny, bloated piece of shit basically given blank checks to spend as idiotically as possible.

"But then why are they genuinely competent at the other stuff"

I can't speak on any definitive canon, but in real life, a lot of the military's tech piggybacks off of government funded research that isn't always even associated with the military right away to start with.

So basically, this explanation posits two different "we":

Government scientists who did all the hard work of actually making their advanced tech work properly, and then the military who swoops in and takes all the glory, making them seem more competent and better managed than they really are.

Although as a little side quibble, by Avatar's time, the government is probably merged with a bunch of corporate crap to the point that it'd be hard to untangle what exactly counts as "private" or "public" research.

replies.text:
Here's a better third movie:
- The Ash Tribe is suffering because Eywa wants them dead
- Quaritch comes to them and offers to help them with human technology- teach them farming, civilize them, etc. 
- The other Na'Vi tribes who are not as favored by Eywa take notice and form alliances with the humans as well
- Sully notices this and starts trying to recruit other Na'Vi as well
- This leads to violent clashes between the two factions of Na'Vi
- Quaritch realizes that Eywa is a real entity that controls everything on the planet and tries to free the Na'Vi from their willing slavery
- This causes religious schism across the planet as the Na'Vi consider how cruel Eywa had been.  Many sign up with Quaritch's faction
- Then end it all with a big battle where the Pro-Human Na'Vi win and drive out the Pro-Eywa Na'Vi, who are forced into hiding in a downer Empire Strikes back sort of ending

replies.author: @John.McMillan
replies.text:
​​@lordshang8838 Alright then speaking on colonialism in general; what is wrong?

There is not a single "peaceful tribe" that has been conquered, it's not like war is unique to the colonial power. These tribes migrate and war all the time and have completely wiped countless of the Earth that we don't know of and many that only exist in tales of conquest, and they do it for the exact same reason - resources, land and security.

Now, I'm not saying some heinous stuff hasn't been done on colonies, Leopold's Congo is plenty example. But the idea of colonization in general is not inherently negative in any way.

For the context of the movies here: Earth is pretty much dry of resources and is poisoned by industry and over mining, so the colonization is a desperate grasp at survival, nothing evil about it.

Think of it with the following nuance;
 If someone robs a church you take service in, they are commiting a truly evil act.
However, if you and yours are starving, so you decide to take the collection of the Church and run, would call yourself evil?

The same can be expounded to a colony. Your homeland is crowded, expensive, and with little chance of prosperity, however joining a colony expedition grants promise, freedom and payment.
On the national scale, your land is already being mines, farmed and harvested to it's maximum yet the requirements of your ever growing population far exceeds your economic capacity, so you go to new lands to fill this constantly growing need.
This is not evil, it's survival.

VS

Your tribe has been hunting and roaming these lands since before your grandfather, and now these new strange people come from beyond the horizon in the sea, establish permanent buildings and rapidly grow beyond the size of your tribe. They take the forests and kill more animals in a month than your tribe can in a year, and they grow more in number now pushing your own territory. You tell them to leave and harass the ones that venture too far, even calling war parties against them.
They then come together, larger than even your war band, and hunt you down and burn your huts, forcing you from your land, something no other tribe managed to do and the only enemy your tribe hasn't been able to beat and conquer.
This is not just survival, it's evil.
